pmos

Harness Engineering for AI — Primer

Created using pmos‑learnkit

Primer · Cross-Functional Skills. A large language model — the kind of AI behind ChatGPT, Claude, and Gemini — answers one prompt and then stops. It cannot click a button, look something up, remember yesterday, or check its own work. Harness engineering is the discipline of building everything around the model that turns it from a text generator into a working product: the loop that lets it act step by step, the tools it can call, the memory and state it carries, the guardrails that keep it safe, and the evaluation harnesses that tell you whether it works. A useful shorthand from Hugging Face captures it — "Agent = Model + Harness." This primer is the map of that wrapper. It is deliberately not about how a model thinks inside (that is a different topic) or about what text you feed it (that is context engineering); it is about the runtime software that makes the model useful and reliable. It opens with what harness engineering is and why a raw model is not a product, then draws the key line between predefined "workflows" and self-directing "agents." It walks the agent loop (the reason-act-observe cycle), tool use, control flow and orchestration, state and memory, guardrails, evaluation harnesses, and observability — the major parts of a harness, one at a time. It then separates harness engineering from its two neighbours (prompt and context engineering), confronts why agents that shine in demos break in production, and ends with what a product manager who owns an agent actually needs to do. Every term of art is defined on first use and every acronym is spelled out — no engineering background assumed. Source provenance: curation-consensus across the practitioner canon — Hugging Face, Anthropic, and the awesome-harness-engineering list for the framing; the ReAct paper and the Prompt Engineering Guide for the loop; LangChain's LangGraph and docs for orchestration and guardrails; Datadog for safety; Anthropic, DeepEval, and LangSmith for evaluation and tracing; Maxim for production reliability; Pragmatic Institute and Product School for the PM lens. This is a young, fast-moving discipline — the term itself is from 2025–2026 — so several framings here are still being argued over. 17 verified sources.

What harness engineering is

Begin with the single fact this whole field is built on: a large language model, on its own, does exactly one thing. A large language model (LLM) is the kind of artificial intelligence that predicts text — the engine inside assistants like ChatGPT, Claude, and Gemini. You give it a prompt, it produces a response, and then it is done. It does not loop, it does not act, it does not remember the last conversation, and it cannot reach out to a database or a web page. Everything that makes a model feel like a capable assistant — taking steps, using tools, remembering — happens outside the model.

That outside layer is the harness. Hugging Face's agent glossary gives the cleanest definition: the harness is "the execution layer inside the agent: it calls the model, handles its tool calls, decides when to stop." Its headline equation is worth memorizing — "Agent = Model + Harness." An agent, it explains, is "a model plus everything around it that lets it act, not just respond ... taking in information, deciding what to do, and acting on the results." The model is the brain; the harness is the body, the senses, and the nervous system that let the brain do anything in the world.

Harness engineering is the discipline of building that wrapper well. The community-maintained awesome-harness-engineering list defines it as "the discipline of designing the scaffolding — context delivery, tool interfaces, planning artifacts, verification loops, memory systems, and sandboxes — that surrounds an AI agent and determines whether it succeeds or fails on real tasks." Its scope, in the list's own words, spans "tools, patterns, evals, memory, MCP, permissions, observability, and orchestration." And it makes the load-bearing point explicit: "This list focuses on the harness, not the model. Every component here exists because the model can't do it alone."

The term is new — it spread through 2025 and 2026 as teams discovered that their hardest problems lived in this wrapper, not in the model. The technologist Adnan Masood calls the harness "the runtime software infrastructure that acts as the operating system around the model," and predicts it will grow into an "Enterprise AI Control Plane, resembling a blend of CI/CD, Identity and Access Management (IAM), and observability." Because the field is this young, the vocabulary is still settling — treat the terms here as the current consensus, not a finished standard.

Why a raw model isn't a product

It is tempting to think that a powerful enough model is the product, and the rest is plumbing. The practitioners building these systems argue the opposite, often bluntly. Adnan Masood titles his central section "Your Model Isn't the Problem — Your Harness Is," reporting that in his analysis the large majority of enterprise AI failures trace back to harness-level defects rather than the model's reasoning. Whether or not that exact proportion holds everywhere, the direction is the consistent message of the field: when an agent misbehaves, the wrapper is usually the culprit.

Why would that be? Because the things a product needs — reliability, safety, the ability to act, the ability to recover from a mistake — are exactly the things a bare model lacks. A model can write a convincing answer, but it cannot guarantee that answer is safe to send, cannot fetch the fact it is missing, cannot retry when a step fails, and cannot tell you tomorrow whether it is still working as well as today. Each of those is a harness job.

This is also why the same frontier model can power a delightful product in one company and a frustrating one in another. The model is increasingly a shared commodity — many teams call the very same model through an API (an application programming interface, the standard way one piece of software calls another). DeepEval, a company that builds evaluation tooling, draws the conclusion sharply: "when everyone has access to roughly the same frontier model, the harness around it is what's left to differentiate on." If the brain is the same for everyone, the body you build around it is your product.

Workflows versus agents

Not every system built on a model needs the full machinery of an agent, and one of the most useful distinctions in the field separates two ways of wiring a model into software. Anthropic, the company behind the Claude models, draws the line in its widely cited guide. A workflow, it writes, describes "systems where LLMs and tools are orchestrated through predefined code paths." An agent, by contrast, describes "systems where LLMs dynamically direct their own processes and tool usage, maintaining control over how they accomplish tasks."

The difference is who decides the steps. In a workflow, a human engineer writes the sequence in advance — first summarize, then classify, then route — and the model fills in each blank. The path is fixed; only the contents vary. In an agent, the model itself decides what to do next at each turn, choosing tools and steps on the fly. Anthropic's compact definition of an agent is "LLMs using tools based on environmental feedback in a loop."

This matters for harness design because the two demand different wrappers. A workflow harness encodes the steps in ordinary code, which makes it predictable and easy to debug. An agent harness must instead manage an open-ended loop, which is more flexible but far harder to keep reliable. Anthropic's practical counsel is to reach for the simpler option first: the most successful implementations, it found, use "simple, composable patterns rather than complex frameworks," because heavy frameworks "often create extra layers of abstraction that can obscure the underlying prompts and responses, making them harder to debug." Not every problem needs an agent; many need only a workflow.

The agent loop

The beating heart of an agent harness is the loop — the cycle that lets a one-shot model take many steps toward a goal. The dominant pattern has a name and a paper behind it: ReAct, short for "Reasoning and Acting," introduced in a 2023 research paper. The idea is to interleave the model's thinking with its doing. As the ReAct project describes it, the approach generates "both reasoning traces and task-specific actions in an interleaved manner ... reasoning traces help the model induce, track, and update action plans as well as handle exceptions, while actions allow it to interface with external sources, such as knowledge bases or environments, to gather additional information."

In practice the loop runs as a repeating three-beat cycle. The Prompt Engineering Guide describes ReAct as running "multiple thought-action-observation steps": the model produces a thought (what should I do next?), takes an action (call a tool), and receives an observation (the tool's result) — then loops back and thinks again, now armed with the new information. The harness is what runs this loop: it reads the model's chosen action, actually executes the tool, feeds the result back in, and decides when the goal is met and the loop should stop.

The reason this pattern won is that reasoning and acting strengthen each other. The ReAct work showed it produces "human-like task-solving trajectories that are more interpretable than baselines," and — crucially — that letting the model check the world reduces "hallucination and error propagation" (a hallucination is when a model states something false with confidence). A model reasoning alone can talk itself into a wrong answer; a model that can act gets to check reality at each step. The harness is what makes that checking possible.

Tools and function calling

If the loop is the heart, tools are the hands. A tool is any external capability the model can invoke — a web search, a database query, a calculator, sending an email, running code. The mechanism is often called function calling: the model emits a structured request to run a named function with specific inputs, and the harness actually executes it and returns the result. Without tools, an agent can only talk; with them, it can do.

Anthropic frames tools as "a new kind of software which reflects a contract between deterministic systems and non-deterministic agents." That phrasing repays a moment's thought. A traditional program is deterministic — same input, same output, every time. A model is non-deterministic — it may phrase or decide things differently from run to run. A tool is the carefully designed handshake between those two worlds, and its quality bounds the whole agent. Anthropic puts it plainly: "agents are only as effective as the tools we give them."

This is why tool design is a real engineering discipline, not an afterthought. Poorly designed tools produce a recognizable family of failures — Anthropic lists agents that "call the wrong tools, call the right tools with the wrong parameters, call too few tools, or process tool responses incorrectly." The fix is to design tools for the model's benefit rather than the engineer's habits: "instead of writing tools and MCP servers the way we'd write functions and APIs for other developers or systems, we need to design them for agents." (MCP, the Model Context Protocol, is an emerging standard way for agents to connect to tools and data — useful to recognize, not essential here.) A clear name, a sharp description, and sensible inputs do more for agent reliability than almost anything else in the harness.

Control flow and orchestration

Once an agent can loop and call tools, the harness needs to manage where the flow goes — when to branch, when to repeat, when to hand off to another agent, when to pause for a human. This is orchestration, and it is where dedicated frameworks have grown up. The most widely used is LangGraph, which models an agent as a graph: a set of steps and the connections between them.

LangGraph provides, in its own description, "low-level primitives" to "create fully customizable agents" with "diverse control flows — single, multi-agent, hierarchical." The graph model is appealing because it makes the otherwise-invisible flow of an agent explicit and inspectable: you can see the steps, the branches, and the loops laid out, rather than buried in a tangle of conditional code. Anthropic's caution from earlier applies here too, though — frameworks add power but also abstraction, and the right amount of orchestration is the minimum the task actually needs.

A central concern of orchestration is keeping a human in control of an agent that is otherwise acting on its own. LangGraph builds this in: "Prevent agents from veering off course with easy-to-add moderation and quality controls. Add human-in-the-loop checks to steer and approve agent actions." A human-in-the-loop check pauses the agent at a sensitive step — say, before it sends money or emails a customer — and waits for a person to approve. For any agent whose actions are hard to reverse, this pause is not a nicety; it is the difference between a helpful tool and a liability.

State and memory

A loop is only useful if the agent remembers what happened on the previous turn, and across a long task that memory has to be actively managed. The harness is what holds the agent's state — the running record of what has been done, what tools returned, and where the task stands — and carries it from one loop iteration to the next.

In LangGraph's graph model, this is a first-class concept: state is the shared data structure that every step reads from and writes to, threaded through the whole run. Its "built-in memory stores conversation histories and maintains context over time." Memory in an agent splits, as it does for any assistant, into the short-term (the current task's running state) and the long-term (durable facts carried across separate sessions, so the agent can remember a user's preferences or past decisions weeks later).

The hard part is that state cannot simply grow forever. Every tool result and every step adds to what the model must carry, and a model has a finite window of text it can consider at once. So a real harness has to curate state — keeping what matters, summarizing or dropping the rest — which is the seam where harness engineering hands off to context engineering (covered as its own neighbour below). For now the key point is that managing state is a harness responsibility: the model has no memory of its own, so if the agent remembers anything, it is because the harness held onto it.

Guardrails and safety

An agent that can act in the world can also act badly — leak private data, follow a malicious instruction, send something it should not. Guardrails are the harness components that prevent this, by checking what goes into and comes out of the model. Datadog, a monitoring company, defines them as mechanisms that "create boundaries around the acceptable inputs and behavior of generative AI applications to enforce security, safety, and compliance in LLM interactions."

Guardrails work at two ends. On the way in, they perform "User input validation (e.g., rejecting adversarial or off-topic user prompts)"; on the way out, "LLM output filtering (e.g., protecting system prompt or training data leakage)." They run "before, during, and after the ingestion of an input prompt," sitting between the user and the model. One threat they exist to stop is prompt injection — an attacker hiding instructions inside ordinary-looking input to hijack the agent's behavior.

Mechanically, LangChain's documentation describes guardrails as middleware — code that wraps the agent's execution: "You can implement guardrails using middleware to intercept execution at strategic points - before the agent starts, after it completes, or around model and tool calls." Before-agent hooks handle "authentication, rate limiting, or blocking inappropriate requests"; after-agent hooks run "final compliance scans on the complete agent response." There are two flavors: deterministic guardrails using "rule-based logic like regex patterns, keyword matching, or explicit checks," which are fast and predictable, and model-based guardrails using "LLMs or classifiers to evaluate content with semantic understanding," which catch subtler problems but cost more. Most serious systems use both.

Evaluation harnesses

You cannot improve what you cannot measure, and because a model's output varies from run to run, measuring an agent is its own engineering problem. An evaluation — almost always shortened to eval — is, in Anthropic's words, "a test for an AI system: give an AI an input, then apply grading logic to its output to measure success." An evaluation harness is the surrounding machinery that runs many such tests automatically. DeepEval calls it "the validation layer for AI agents": it loops through a set of known-good test cases, captures "the response, and execution trace if available," and runs "your metric suite on it" to produce a score you can compare over time.

The reason evals are non-negotiable is the alternative. Anthropic warns that without them "it's easy to get stuck in reactive loops—catching issues only in production, where fixing one failure creates others." A particularly important kind is the regression eval, which asks whether a change quietly broke something that used to work. Anthropic notes these "should have a nearly 100% pass rate ... a decline in score signals that something is broken." Evals, done well, make "problems and behavioral changes visible before they affect users."

One subtle distinction is worth flagging because it is easy to confuse. An eval and a guardrail both check an agent's output, but they live in different places. DeepEval draws the line: "The moment it's in the live path and gates the response, that's a guardrail, not an eval." A guardrail runs in production, on every real request, blocking bad output before a user sees it. An eval runs offline, during development, telling you whether a new version is better or worse before you ship it. Same kind of check, opposite ends of the lifecycle — and a healthy harness needs both.

Observability and tracing

When an agent does something wrong, the first question is: where did it go wrong? An agent takes many hidden steps — thoughts, tool calls, observations — and the final answer reveals almost nothing about the path that produced it. Observability is the harness layer that records that path so a human can inspect it, and the core technique is tracing — capturing every step of a run.

LangSmith, an observability and evaluation platform, describes the capability directly: its agent evaluation "captures the full trajectory of steps, tool calls, and reasoning your agent took." A trajectory is the complete sequence the agent moved through — every thought, every action, every result. With it captured, teams can "Define evaluators that score intermediate decisions and agent behavior to debug complex agent workflows and pinpoint where things went wrong."

The deeper point is that evaluating only an agent's final answer is not enough — two agents can reach the same answer by wildly different routes, one sound and one lucky. Capturing the trajectory is what lets a team "identify failure points in multi-turn workflows" rather than just noticing that the end result looks off. Observability is to an agent what logging and monitoring are to ordinary software, adapted to the fact that the thing being watched reasons in steps you cannot see from the outside.

Harness engineering versus prompt and context engineering

Harness engineering has two close neighbours it is easy to blur together, and keeping them straight clarifies what each actually buys you. Prompt engineering is the craft of wording a single request well. Context engineering is the craft of deciding what information to put in front of the model — the instructions, retrieved documents, examples, and history. Harness engineering is the craft of building the runtime around the model — the loop, tools, guardrails, and evals. Roughly: prompt engineering is what you say, context engineering is what you show, and harness engineering is what you build.

The practitioner case is that, for production systems, the harness layer carries more weight than prompt wording. AI Magicx frames harness engineering as "the execution environment, agent scaffolding, and orchestration logic around an LLM ... the new discipline that separates toy demos from production AI systems," covering "everything around the LLM call: the execution environment, the tool integrations, the memory system, the retry logic, the guardrails, the context assembly pipeline, the output validation." It cites a Stanford HAI finding that "prompt refinement beyond a reasonable baseline improved output quality by less than 3%" while "Harness-level changes -- adding retrieval, tool access, and structured validation -- improved quality by 28-47%."

A word of caution: this framing is partly an argument, not just a fact, and the more breathless versions ("prompt engineering is dead") oversell it. Prompting and context still matter — a good harness around a badly worded prompt is no better than its inputs. The honest reading is that the three are layers of one job, and that as systems move from demo to production, the center of gravity shifts outward from the prompt to the harness. Which layer your effort should go into depends on where your system is losing quality — and the only way to know that is to measure, which is why evals and observability sit at the core of the discipline.

Reliability in production

Everything so far converges on a single hard problem: an agent that dazzles in a demo often crumbles in production, and the harness is where that gap is won or lost. Maxim, a company building agent-reliability tooling, states the stakes directly: "The gap between AI agents that work in demos and agents that work reliably in production determines which organizations will succeed with AI automation."

What makes agents uniquely hard to keep reliable is the nature of their failures. Ordinary software fails loudly — it throws an error, it crashes, an alarm goes off. Agents fail quietly. As Maxim puts it, "Unlike traditional applications that fail with clear error messages, AI agents frequently experience silent quality degradation." An agent can keep producing fluent, confident, plausible answers that are slowly getting worse, and nothing in the system raises a flag. The same model behind it can shift, the data it relies on can drift, and the outputs degrade without a single visible error.

This is why Maxim argues that "Traditional software reliability approaches prove inadequate for managing probabilistic systems where failures often manifest as subtle quality degradation rather than clear system errors." The remedy is precisely the harness components this primer has walked through, working together: guardrails to catch bad output in the live path, observability to see what the agent actually did, and evals to detect when quality slips. Reliability is not a feature you add at the end; it is the property that emerges when the whole harness is built to expect that the model will sometimes be wrong.

What this means for product managers

For a product manager who owns an AI agent, the lesson of harness engineering is a reframing of where the work — and the risk — actually lives. The instinct is to treat a disappointing agent as a model problem, to be solved by waiting for a smarter model or polishing the prompt. The consistent message of the field is that most of the leverage is in the harness: the tools, the guardrails, the evals, the structure around the model. When the agent is unreliable, look first at the wrapper you built.

That puts real responsibilities on the PM, and they are not the glamorous ones. Pragmatic Institute is blunt: "Agents are only as effective as the structure behind them." The structure the PM owns is the specification — "clearly define the goal, not just the task," "define each step of the task along the way," and "be explicit about what data can be used, what actions are allowed, and how you want the output to look." Those three — goal, steps, constraints — map almost one to one onto the harness's loop, tools, and guardrails. The PM's clarity becomes the agent's reliability: "Without structure, agents produce output. With structure, they produce something useful."

It also demands a posture of permanent oversight. Product School advises treating an agent's work as provisional: "Even great AI agents need oversight. Treat their outputs like drafts, not final answers," and, memorably, to "treat AI agents like Associate Product Managers. They can get a lot done, but they still need guidance." Concretely, that means the PM should insist on the harness having evals before launch and observability after it, should define the guardrails for anything customer-facing or hard to reverse, and should expect to watch for the silent degradation that ordinary dashboards will not surface. The harness is not the engineering team's private concern — its shape is a product decision, and owning it is now part of the job.

Where this connects — adjacent topics

  • Context engineering for AI — the sibling discipline of deciding what information fills the model's window (retrieval, prompts, memory, tool results). Harness engineering builds the runtime; context engineering curates what flows through it.
  • How large language models work — the model internals this primer treats as a black box: tokens, attention, and why a model predicts text. The harness wraps that engine.
  • Prompt engineering — the narrowest of the three layers: wording a single request well.
  • AI agents — the broader topic of autonomous systems that plan and act; harness engineering is how you actually build a reliable one.
  • AI evaluation — the measurement discipline that evals and observability draw on, deepened into datasets, metrics, and LLM-as-judge scoring.
  • AI safety and guardrails — the security and alignment concerns behind the guardrail layer, including prompt injection and data protection.

References

  1. huggingface.co ·cited· (T1)

    Hugging Face's agent glossary gives the cleanest split: the harness is "the execution layer inside the agent: it calls the model, handles its tool calls, decides when to stop," while scaffolding is "the behavior-defining layer around the model: system prompt, tool descriptions, how the model's responses get parsed, what it remembers across steps." Its headline equation is "Agent = Model + Harness" — "An agent is a model plus everything around it that lets it act, not just respond ... taking in information, deciding what to do, and acting on the results."

  2. anthropic.com ·cited· (T1)

    Anthropic distinguishes workflows — "systems where LLMs and tools are orchestrated through predefined code paths" — from agents — "systems where LLMs dynamically direct their own processes and tool usage, maintaining control over how they accomplish tasks." Agents are "LLMs using tools based on environmental feedback in a loop." Its core advice: prefer "simple, composable patterns rather than complex frameworks," since frameworks "often create extra layers of abstraction that can obscure the underlying prompts and responses, making them harder to debug."

  3. react-lm.github.io ·cited· (T1)

    The ReAct project (ICLR 2023) combines reasoning and acting: it generates "both reasoning traces and task-specific actions in an interleaved manner ... reasoning traces help the model induce, track, and update action plans as well as handle exceptions, while actions allow it to interface with external sources, such as knowledge bases or environments, to gather additional information." The result is "human-like task-solving trajectories that are more interpretable than baselines," and it reduces "hallucination and error propagation" by interacting with a real tool.

  4. anthropic.com ·cited· (T1)

    Anthropic frames tools as "a new kind of software which reflects a contract between deterministic systems and non-deterministic agents," and warns that "agents are only as effective as the tools we give them." Poor tool design produces specific failures — agents may "call the wrong tools, call the right tools with the wrong parameters, call too few tools, or process tool responses incorrectly" — so "instead of writing tools ... the way we'd write functions and APIs for other developers ... we need to design them for agents."

  5. langchain.com ·cited· (T1)

    LangGraph provides "low-level primitives" to "create fully customizable agents" with "diverse control flows — single, multi-agent, hierarchical." Its "built-in memory stores conversation histories and maintains context over time," and it supports governance: "Prevent agents from veering off course with easy-to-add moderation and quality controls. Add human-in-the-loop checks to steer and approve agent actions," plus "native token-by-token streaming, showing agent reasoning and actions in real time."

  6. datadoghq.com ·cited· (T1)

    Datadog defines guardrails as mechanisms that "create boundaries around the acceptable inputs and behavior of generative AI applications to enforce security, safety, and compliance in LLM interactions." They operate as "User input validation (e.g., rejecting adversarial or off-topic user prompts)" and "LLM output filtering (e.g., protecting system prompt or training data leakage)," functioning "before, during, and after the ingestion of an input prompt" — sitting between user and model to block prompt injection and data exfiltration.

  7. docs.langchain.com ·cited· (T1)

    LangChain's guardrails docs show guardrails as middleware: "You can implement guardrails using middleware to intercept execution at strategic points - before the agent starts, after it completes, or around model and tool calls." Before-agent hooks handle "authentication, rate limiting, or blocking inappropriate requests"; after-agent hooks do "final compliance scans on the complete agent response." Two styles exist — deterministic ("rule-based logic like regex patterns") and model-based ("LLMs or classifiers to evaluate content with semantic understanding").

  8. anthropic.com ·cited· (T1)

    Anthropic defines an evaluation ("eval") as "a test for an AI system: give an AI an input, then apply grading logic to its output to measure success." Evals matter because "without them, it's easy to get stuck in reactive loops—catching issues only in production, where fixing one failure creates others." Regression evals "should have a nearly 100% pass rate ... a decline in score signals that something is broken," making "problems and behavioral changes visible before they affect users."

  9. deepeval.com ·cited· (T1)

    DeepEval calls an evaluation harness "the validation layer for AI agents" — it loops through a dataset of golden test cases, captures "the response, and execution trace if available," and runs "your metric suite on it" to score. It draws a sharp line: "The moment it's in the live path and gates the response, that's a guardrail, not an eval" (evals run offline in development). Its thesis: "when everyone has access to roughly the same frontier model, the harness around it is what's left to differentiate on."

  10. langchain.com ·cited· (T1)

    LangSmith's agent evaluation "captures the full trajectory of steps, tool calls, and reasoning your agent took," letting teams "Define evaluators that score intermediate decisions and agent behavior to debug complex agent workflows and pinpoint where things went wrong." Because agents take many hidden steps, evaluating only the final output is not enough — capturing each step is how teams "identify failure points in multi-turn workflows."

  11. pragmaticinstitute.com ·cited· (T1)

    Pragmatic Institute tells PMs that "Agents are only as effective as the structure behind them." The job is to "clearly define the goal, not just the task," to "define each step of the task along the way," and to "be explicit about what data can be used, what actions are allowed, and how you want the output to look." Its bottom line: "Without structure, agents produce output. With structure, they produce something useful," because "the quality of the output is directly tied to the clarity of the instruction."

  12. medium.com ·cited· (T2)

    Adnan Masood defines the agent harness as "the scaffolding around an LLM that turns it into a working agent — the loop, tool calls, context management, memory, guardrails, and tracing," or "the runtime software infrastructure that acts as the operating system around the model." His provocation: "Your Model Isn't the Problem — Your Harness Is," arguing that the harness is becoming an "Enterprise AI Control Plane, resembling a blend of CI/CD, Identity and Access Management (IAM), and observability."

  13. github.com ·cited· (T2)

    The awesome-harness-engineering list defines harness engineering as "the discipline of designing the scaffolding — context delivery, tool interfaces, planning artifacts, verification loops, memory systems, and sandboxes — that surrounds an AI agent and determines whether it succeeds or fails on real tasks." Its scope spans "tools, patterns, evals, memory, MCP, permissions, observability, and orchestration," with the framing note: "This list focuses on the harness, not the model. Every component here exists because the model can't do it alone."

  14. promptingguide.ai ·cited· (T2)

    The Prompt Engineering Guide describes ReAct as a loop: "ReAct prompts LLMs to generate verbal reasoning traces and actions for a task," running "multiple thought-action-observation steps" where the model reasons, acts, receives an observation, and continues. It allows "interaction to external environments ... to incorporate additional information into the reasoning," so the model can "perform dynamic reasoning to create, maintain, and adjust plans for acting."

  15. getmaxim.ai ·cited· (T2)

    Maxim argues that "The gap between AI agents that work in demos and agents that work reliably in production determines which organizations will succeed with AI automation." The hard part is that "Unlike traditional applications that fail with clear error messages, AI agents frequently experience silent quality degradation" — so "Traditional software reliability approaches prove inadequate for managing probabilistic systems where failures often manifest as subtle quality degradation rather than clear system errors," demanding observability, continuous evaluation, and purpose-built error handling.

  16. aimagicx.com ·cited· (T2)

    AI Magicx frames harness engineering as "the execution environment, agent scaffolding, and orchestration logic around an LLM ... the new discipline that separates toy demos from production AI systems" — "everything around the LLM call: the execution environment, the tool integrations, the memory system, the retry logic, the guardrails, the context assembly pipeline, the output validation." It cites a Stanford HAI finding that "prompt refinement beyond a reasonable baseline improved output quality by less than 3%" while "Harness-level changes -- adding retrieval, tool access, and structured validation -- improved quality by 28-47%."

  17. productschool.com ·cited· (T2)

    Product School defines AI agents as "software systems that leverage artificial intelligence capabilities to offer autonomous action, logical reasoning, and continuous learning," able to "string together multiple actions — planning and executing a sequence" and "make decisions on the fly." But it stresses oversight: "Even great AI agents need oversight. Treat their outputs like drafts, not final answers," and "treat AI agents like Associate Product Managers. They can get a lot done, but they still need guidance."

pmos Source: docs/pmos/primer/2026-06-14_harness-engineering-for-ai.html · Created using pmos‑learnkit
